pkgmetadata.longdescription:
Firejail is a SUID program that reduces the risk of security breaches by restricting the running environment of untrusted applications using Linux namespaces and seccomp-bpf. It allows a process and all its descendants to have their own private view of the globally shared kernel resources, such as the network stack, process table, mount table.
This is the regular version. For a long term support version see sys-apps/firejail-lts.
pkgmetadata.ebuild-documentation:
oiledmachine-overlay ebuild changes and ebuild additions:
- Improved Xpra support - Enable or disable sound forwarding - /usr/local/bin wrapper auto generation - ROP attack mitigations - Support for hardened mallocs
Ebuild fork goals:
- Minimize install bloat - Make it easier to use program sandboxed - Sandboxed on install without user intervention - Least privileges corrections
Wrapper versus symlink
Firejail can use symlink from /usr/bin/firefail to /usr/local/bin/<cli-name>, but it runs /usr/bin/<cli-name> with <cli-name> only if /usr/local/bin/<cli-name> is the same as /usr/bin/<cli-name>. Some programs, however, cannot be paired correctly because the profile name is unconventional or has a capitalization issue.
This is why a shell wrapper script is used instead. The shell wrapper will tell Firejail to run the correct /usr/local/bin/<cli-name> wrapper with /usr/bin/<cli-name>. These compatiblity wrappers will be autogenerated by the ebuild. So correctness increases but the startup latency will decrease a little because of the shell script startup cost.
Sandbox and cybersecurity terminology
Privileged - outside the sandbox or exposure to sensitive data, tools, actions
Unprivileged - inside the sandbox or no exposure to sensitive data, tools, actions
Degraded capabilities - reducing the threat actor to unprivileged
Drop zone - a staging place where threat actors place their tools
Command and control (C2) - a compromised computer and obfuscated attacker location that acts as a master node to send control commands to zombies
Zombie - a compromised computer used as a slave node that does the dirty work
Mitigated threats when blacklists are properly used for globals.local:
- Covert channels - blacklist /sys/class/backlight # (PoC) BRIGHTNESS attack - Data tampering - Data theft - Doxxing - Drop zones and zombie installation - blacklist /home - blacklist /media - blacklist /mnt - Extortion - Information disclosure - Living of the land (LotL) - Path traversal - Ransomware - Rubberhose attack
Mitigated threats when sandbox restrictions are properly used for <cli-name>.local:
- Drop zones and zombie installation - disable-mnt - Privilege escalation - nonewprivs for SUID mitigation - noroot for removing the root account inside the sandbox - seccomp and caps.drop=all for kernel attack surface reduction and sandbox breakout mitigation
The *.local files are used to degrade the capabilities of threat actors or to fix poorly written Firejail profiles. The *.local files are a proper layered defense fallback and security baseline.
Important blacklist paths
IMPORTANT! You must verify that your crown jewels, your keys to the crown jewels, your sensitive data is unreachable and unreadable by any attacker. The default profiles do not necessarily mean information disclosure is mitigated. Custom rules must be added for full mitigation of information disclosure.
To get the full list of file names for <cli-name>.local, do `grep -r -e "\.local" /etc/firejail`. Intuitively, it is the same name as the cli command.
Ordered by precedence
1. ~/.config/firejail/<cli-name>.local - is your personal per program blacklist or config for edge cases. (Highest precedence, overwrites others)
2. /etc/firejail/<cli-name>.local - is a systemwide per program blacklist or config for edge cases.
3. ~/.config/firejail/globals.local - is a per-user profile blacklist containing paths in ${HOME} or user mounted paths to prevent a path traversal exfiltration, but if the profile is compromised then it won't work as effective as /etc/firejail/globals.local. This private list is to prevent snooping by users on the same computer.
4. /etc/firejail/globals.local - is systemwide configuration containing all blacklisted paths having sensitive data (e.g. API keys, AI keys, server keys). (Lowest precedence, evaluated first)
Examples:
# DISCLAIMER: These are fictional/hypothetical examples. You must verify # the actual paths.
Contents of /etc/firejail/globals.local:
# Using ls /backups will result in access denied when attempting to access # the crown jewels or the keys to the crown jewels blacklist /backups blacklist /mnt/usb/keys # Keys to the crown jewels blacklist /mnt/usb/pictures # Crown jewels, keys, or sensitive data
# # The general categories at high risk or have threat actors: # Contents of ~/.config/firejail/<cli-name>.local:
# Anonymous networks Contents of ~/.config/firejail/i2prouter.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/tor.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/torbrowser-launcher.local:
# Cryptocurrency Contents of ~/.config/firejail/bitcoin-qt.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/electrum.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/monero-wallet-cli.local:
# Document viewers Contents of ~/.config/firejail/evince.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/libreoffice.local:
# Downloaders or media players Contents of ~/.config/firejail/celluloid.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/deluge.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/filezilla.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/gnome-mplayer.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/ktorrent.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/kmplayer.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/mplayer.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/mpv.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/npm.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/pnpm.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/qbittorrent.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/rtorrent.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/smplayer.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/transmission-common.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/vlc.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/yarn.local:
# E-mail clients Contents of ~/.config/firejail/evolution.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/thunderbird.local:
# General high threats Contents of ~/.config/firejail/electron-common.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/nodejs-common.local:
# Web browsers # You may use the -common variants for derived versions. Contents of ~/.config/firejail/brave.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/chromium.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/chromium-common.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/dillo.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/ephemeral.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/epiphany.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/falkon.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/firefox.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/firefox-common.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/google-chrome.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/librewolf.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/lynx.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/microsoft-edge.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/midori.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/min.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/mullvad-browser.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/netsurf.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/opera.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/otter-browser.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/surf.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/uzbl-browser.local:
# Secure private chat or collaborative messaging Contents of ~/.config/firejail/element-desktop.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/signal-desktop.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/slack.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/telegram.local: Contents of ~/.config/firejail/zoom.local:
# Protect against using /home/<user-name> as a drop zone or against exfiltration # for the above high threat profiles of your choosing. Keep protected 99% # of the time, # and permit 1% of the time. blacklist /home
Contents of ~/.config/firejail/globals.local:
# Use the AI to help locate and blacklist all high value asset credentials.
# Most of the time these will be blacklisted. Sometimes you may need to # temporary disable the blacklist. After use, re-enable the blacklist rule.
# 99% of the time the blacklist will be enabled. 1% of the time the rule # will be disabled.
blacklist /mnt/usb/password # Keys to the crown jewels blacklist /mnt/usb/assets # Crown jewels or sensitive data
# Protect cryptocurrency wallets blacklist /mnt/usb/wallet blacklist ${HOME}/.bitcoin blacklist ${HOME}/.electrum blacklist ${HOME}/.ethereum blacklist ${HOME}/.monero
# Protect config and credentials blacklist ${HOME}/.config/solana blacklist ${HOME}/.config/ledger-live-desktop blacklist ${HOME}/.config/TreZor
# Protect SSH keys and secrets blacklist ${HOME}/.ssh blacklist ${HOME}/.gnupg blacklist ${HOME}/.password-store
# Protect license files blacklist ${HOME}/.local/share/unity3d/Unity blacklist ${HOME}/.local/share/unity3d blacklist ${HOME}/.config/unity blacklist ${HOME}/.config/unity3d
# Protect addresses and PII (Personal Identifiable Information) blacklist ${HOME}/Documents blacklist ${HOME}/Pictures
# Option 1 - Blacklisting downloads manually. # Add the comment character # before the command below if you want to # download temporarily. Forgetting to enable the blacklist can result in # a information disclosure vulnerability. The download directory is # protected to prevent data theft of PII or sensitive data when the # browser sandbox breakout happens. For 99% of the time, the Download # folder rule containing PII should be blacklisted.
blacklist ${HOME}/Downloads # Protected folder without # character #blacklist ${HOME}/Downloads # Vulnerable with # character
# Option 2 - Blacklisting downloads with custom wrappers. # You can also add --blacklist=${HOME}/Downloads as the default and have a # separate wrapper firefox-download for downloads. Make a copy first with # `cp -a /usr/local/bin/firefox /usr/local/bin/firefox-download`. # Modify /usr/local/bin/firefox with --blacklist=${HOME}/Downloads and remove # --blacklist=${HOME}/Downloads from /usr/local/bin/firefox-download. # Immediately close the browser and use /usr/local/bin/firefox to prevent # information disclosure based path traversal. Do not apply the # blacklist ${HOME}/Downloads in ~/.config/firejail/globals.local for # this option.
# The custom wrappers should be placed in /usr/local/bin not in ~/ to # prevent compromised profile code execution.
Path traversal mitigation verification
To verify that path traversal is mitigated by the sandbox, use one of these commands:
firejail --profile=<profile-name> --private-bin=bash,<term>,ls,id,head /usr/bin/<term> # For your favorite terminal app firejail --profile=<profile-name> --private-bin=bash,xfce4-terminal,ls,id,head /usr/bin/xfce4-terminal # For X11/Wayland compatibility
Use `head <path>` to test if the file is readable by the attacker. Use `ls <path>` to test if the file is accessible by the attacker. Use `ls *` to print all current directory files and folders which potentially have sensitive data
Your keyfiles should not be reachable when doing this test. If reachable, you need to either move the keys physically on another device and blacklist that abspath dir in ~/.config/firejail/globals.local.
You should also test tools/commands for proper mitigation of the Living of the Land (LotL) attack. See https://gtfobins.org/ for possible commands that can be weaponized by threat actors. Use <cmd> --version to test. If it prints the version or doesn't say command not found, it is considered privileged.
Low hanging fruit to test LotL mitigation:
- awk, grep, sed -- for exfiltration or tampering - bash, perl, php, python, ruby, sh -- scripting commands for automating exploitation - clang, gcc -- compiler commands to build kernel exploits - curl, scp, ssh, ftp, tftp, wget -- network commands to exfiltrate or install exploits in drop zones - dd - for exfiltration, reconaissance, sabotage, destroying attacker evidence - find, ls -- for reconnasiance - tar, gunzip, unzip -- for unpacking exploits
To test ls, you can use a shell script like `bash -c 'test -e <path>; echo $?'`.
The goal is to take away the ability for threat actors to use them or to degrade their use with blacklists or sandbox restrictions.
Build time environment variables for USE=wrapper
If the <cli-name> starts with a number, it must be prefixed with the _ character.
APPARMOR_PROFILE[<cli-name>] - Set app armor protection. Valid values - default, APPARMOR_PROFILE_NAME, unset (default off)
ARGS[<cli-name>] - Add the raw list of args to the wrapper for full control. To properly sandbox the app with COMMAND[<cli-name>], the program must be limited as much as possible.
AUTO_BLACKLIST - Disable auto adding wrapper to /usr/local/bin as a space delimited list Valid values - <cli-name>, unset (permit app)
COMMAND[<cli-name>] - Create a wrapper without a profile. Valid values - 1 (create), 0 (don't create)
The COMMAND[<cli-name>]="1" can be used to increase coverage of sandboxed programs typically those without a <cli-name>. This can use all the VAR_NAME[<cli-name>] environment variables.
FIREJAIL_DEFAULT_WIDTH - Horizontal size Default: 1920 Valid values - Integer, typically 800, 1920, 2560, 3840
FIREJAIL_DEFAULT_HEIGHT - Vertical size Default: 1080 Valid values - Integer, typically 600, 1080, 1440, 2160
FIREJAIL_DEFAULT_BPP - Bits per pixel Default: 24 Valid values - Integer, typically 8, 16, 24, 32
LANDLOCK[<cli-name>] - Restrict app file access. Valid values - 1 (restrict), 0 (unrestrict), unset (unrestrict)
LANDLOCK_PROC[<cli-name>] - Restrict proc Valid values - no, ro, rw, unset (don't apply)
LANDLOCK_READ[<cli-name>] - Add read rule path is comma separated Valid values - PATH1; PATH1,PATH2; PATH1,PATH2,...; unset (don't apply)
LANDLOCK_WRITE[<cli-name>] - Add write rule path is comma separated Valid values - PATH1; PATH1,PATH2; PATH1,PATH2,...; unset (don't apply)
LANDLOCK_EXECUTE[<cli-name>] - Add execute rule path is comma separated Valid values - PATH1; PATH1,PATH2; PATH1,PATH2,...; unset (don't apply)
MALLOC_BACKEND[<cli-name>] - Set the hardened heap allocator Valid values - hardened_malloc, mimalloc, scudo, system-malloc, unset (It will fallback to hardened malloc followed by ptmalloc.)
Heap allocator | HO | OOB | UAF | HF | FB | FP | TC | MTE | Examples --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- bmalloc | y | y | y | n | poison | none | n | n | Older WebKitGTK release jemalloc | n | n | n[11] | n | zero/poison (c)[7][10] | none | n | n | Mono, Blender, MariaDB, QEMU hardened_malloc | y | y | y | y[2] | poison | none | y | y (c) | GrapheneOS libpas | y | y | y | y[1] | none | none | y | n | Latest WebKitGTK mimalloc | y | y | y | y[3] | none | none | n | n | AI/ML (pyTorch, ONNX Runtime) mozjemalloc | n | n | n[11] | n/a | zero/poison (c)[8][10] | none | n | n | Firefox PartitionAlloc | n[12] | n[12] | n[12] | y[5] | poison | none | y | y | Chromium ptmalloc | n | n | n | n | none | none | n | n | glibc, Linux userland apps Scudo | y | y | y | y[4] | zero/poison (c)[9] | none | n | y | Android Slub | y | y | y | y[6] | none | none/poison (c) | n | n | Kernel/drivers tcmalloc | y | y | y | n | none | none | n | n | Databases (Redis, MariaDB)
For sensitive data apps: KASLR, HO=y, OOB=y, UAF=y, FP=poison|zero are required for security-critical. For web browsers: KASLR, HO=y, OOB=y, UAF=y, FP=poison|zero, TC=y are required for security-critical.
HO = Heap Overflow mitigation OOB = Out of Bounds mitigation UAF = Use After Free mitigation HF = Hash Function used for ASLR FB = Freed Block in page wipe policy, especially for sensitive data (The poison random pattern is used to detect memory corruption bugs, not necessarily to secure wipe.) FP = Freed Page wipe policy, (c) = configurable, (o) = optional TC = Type Confusion mitigation MTE = Memory Type Extension (arm64 only), hardware accelerated UAF detection and protection, (c) = if enabled at build time ASLR = Address Space Layout Randomization protection (userspace fallback if kernel was not built with KASLR) [1] = Either Xorshift PRNG or ARC4 [2] = ChaCha8 [3] = ChaCha20 [4] = KASLR required & linear function [5] = ALSR, linear PSNR [6] = via KASLR [7] = It is default off. [8] = It may be off by default. [9] = Poison is default on. [10] = Can use runtime environment variables MALLOC_CONF=junk:true for poison, or MALLOC_CONF=zero:true for zeroing [11] = If poison is enabled, it could detect and mitigate some UAF. The default values make it no. Using zero does not turn on UAF mitigation. [12] = GWP-ASAN is disabled by default on Linux
OOM[<cli-name>] - Set the normalized number of pages for the OOM (Out Of Memory) killer. Higher values mean likely killed based on OOM killer context. Valid values: For root: -1000 to 1000 For non-root: 0 to 1000 -value - deduct the badness score by this amount.
base_score = n_task_ram_pages + n_task_swap_pages + n_page_table_pages # typically < 100 per task badness_value = base_score + n_normalized_pages
PATH_CORRECTION[<cli-name>] - Set the correct path to the binary package.
SCOPE[<cli-name>] - Scope of the wrapper. Valid values
ban - do not generate wrapper (Aliases: blacklist, blacklisted)
Note:
* The SCOPE[<cli-name>] can override the ebuild defaults for ebuilds that use commands that should be in the conflicting set.
SCUDO_FREE_IMMEDIATE[<cli-name>] - Free the keys in the heap immediately. Valid values: 1 (clear immediately for mitigation against cold-boot-attack and DMA based attacks.) 0 (delayed free for mitigation against use-after-free heap attacks.)
SECCOMP[<cli-name>] - Enable seccomp sandboxing with defaults Valid values: 1 (enable), 0 (disable/default), unset (disable)
SECCOMP_BLOCK[<cli-name>] - Enable seccomp with blacklisted syscalls separated with comma Valid values: SYSCALL1; SYSCALL1,SYSCALL2; SYSCALL1,SYSCALL2,...; unset (don't apply)
SECCOMP_KEEP[<cli-name>] - Enable seccomp with whitelisted syscalls separated with comma Valid values: SYSCALL1; SYSCALL1,SYSCALL2; SYSCALL1,SYSCALL2,...; unset (don't apply)
XEPHYR_WH[<cli-name>] - Set the default window size for X11 apps that are sandboxed with the zephyr backend. Valid values: WIDTHxHEIGHT, 800x600 (default)
X_BACKEND[<cli-name>] - Select the X11 sandbox backend. Valid values: unsandboxed - Do not add any flags. Hardware accelerated OpenGL (gaming-unsandbox, opengl-unsandboxed, none, disable are acceptable aliases) xephyr - Runs the program using Xephyr xpra - Runs the program using Xpra (gaming-sandboxed, opengl-sandboxed are acceptable aliases) xvfb - Runs the program using Xvfb (headless, /dev/null are an acceptable aliases) unset - Default as Xpra or Xephyr if X11 app. Otherwise, it will default to unsandboxed X11 for CLI/TUI apps in a virtual terminal. If the CLI/TUI app is running under a virtual terminal emulator with the x-terminal-emulator profile, it will be X11 isolated. However, the tabs might not have isolation among each other.
Backend | Status | Criticality | Security score | Performance score | Flaws [5][6] | Benefits [5][6] | OpenGL support --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- unsandboxed | works | performance-critical | 2 | 10 | no frame grab isolation and | fastest fps, fastest start | yes, fast | | | | | no input isolation | | xephyr | works | security-security | 9 | 8 | window size inconsistencies | fast start, faster scroll | yes, but slow xpra | works | security-critical | 9 | 4 | slow start | no window size problems | yes, but slow xpra-virtualgl | works [2] | balanced-security | 8 | 6 | slow start | no window size problems | yes, but slow xorg [1][3] | works sometimes [4] | balanced-security | 6 | 9 | weak input isolation | partial screenshot protection | yes, fast xvfb | untested | security-critical | 9 | 3 | | hidden output | untested wayland | works | performance-critical & | 10 | 10 | annoying quirks, | fastest fps | yes, fast | | security-critical | | | compatibility | |
Security-critical means 90% or better security score (A grade). Performance-critical means 90% or better performance score (A grade). Balanced is B or C grade (70-89%).
[1] Workarounds may compromise security. Issue 1741 [2] Disabled [3] Known as xcsecurity, x11 security extension [4] Some apps do not work with it [5] Frame grab isolation mitigates the analog hole like leak when using the show password feature typically found in password managers or from taking captures of sensitive documents or numbers. [6] Input isolation mitigates a compromised app from snooping or keylogging on passwords between two X window applications.
Tests performed with WebGL Aquarium and software rendering override
Chromium --disable-gpu no Firejail: 2 fps Chromium no Firejail: 60 fps Chromium with Firejail --x11=xephyr: 11 fps Chromium with Firejail --x11=xpra and opengl=yes: 7 fps Chromium with Firejail --x11=xpra and opengl=no: 7 fps Chromium with Firejail --x11=xpra-virtualgl and opengl=no: 7 fps Chromium with Firejail --x11=xpra-virtualgl and opengl=yes: 7 fps Chromium with Firejail without --x11 (same as X_BACKEND[<cli-name>]=unsanboxed|disable|none): 60 fps
Variables are in all capitals.
Examples:
Contents of /etc/portage/env/firejail.conf:
APPARMOR_PROFILE["chromium"]="chromium-browser" APPARMOR_PROFILE["firefox"]="default" ARGS[_7z]="--noprinters" AUTO_BLACKLIST="node cargo" LANDLOCK["chromium"]=1 LANDLOCK["firefox"]=1 MALLOC_BACKEND["keepassxc"]="mimalloc" MALLOC_BACKEND["mpv"]="scudo" MALLOC_BACKEND["mplayer"]="hardened_malloc" MALLOC_BACKEND["spotify"]="system-malloc" PATH_CORRECTION["spotify"]="/opt/spotify/spotify-client/spotify" SCOPE[cargo]="ban" SCOPE[node]="local" SCUDO_FREE_IMMEDIATE["keepassxc"]=1 SECCOMP["dillo"]=1 XEPHYR_WH["chromium"]="1918x1058" XEPHYR_WH["firefox"]="1918x1058" X_BACKEND["chromium"]="xephyr" X_BACKEND["firefox"]="xpra" X_BACKEND["spotify"]="xephyr" X_BACKEND["xchat"]="auto"
COMMAND["upscayl"]=1 ARGS["upscayl"]="--nosound --noprinters --noprofile" MALLOC_BACKEND["upscayl"]="mimalloc" PATH_CORRECTION["upscayl"]="/usr/bin/upscayl" X_BACKEND["upscayl"]="xpra"
Contents of /etc/portage/package.env:
sys-apps/firejail firejail.conf
Analysis and explanations:
/etc/portage/env/firejail.conf is a fixed path. It should not be customizable. This is to make associative arrays work.
By default in this ebuild, scudo will free memory blocks with a delay. This delay is to mitigate against use-after-free. This delay is dangerous for cold boot attacks or maybe DMA based ones since freeing blocks is not eager but event based on size.
This is why we choose the mimalloc which does eager free. The keychain has to be purged ASAP after the program has been killed without a chance of calling its graceful cleaner routine.
By default, paths are assumed to be /usr/bin/<cli-name>. You may want to fix the profile name paring with PATH_CORRECTION for binary packages.
Auto adding/generating wrappers with USE=auto with USE=wrapper
The auto USE flag can be used to auto add wrappers to /usr/local/bin with AUTO_BLACKLIST[<cli-name>]. This will sandbox more X apps for more cautious people but increase protection and decrease data theft from biased programs. The BLACKLIST is to prevent an unbootable system, prevent a broken package manager, or those those prefer some games/apps to be more performant.
When a program is AUTO_BLACKLIST, it means that it cannot be a wrapper/symlink to /usr/local/bin/<cli-name>, but it is maybe recommened to add a wrapper/symlink to some location in the home dir so the non-root user can increase their sandbox coverage.
What should be sandboxed or wrapped/symlinked to /usr/local/bin/<cli-name>?
1. Proprietary programs which are considered untrusted 2. End of Life programs/versions 3. Binary only programs 4. Network based programs 5. Programs that are capable of loading user generated content 6. Programs that are statically linked to vulnerable libraries 7. Programs that have a unpatched vulnerability 8. Doubious programs
If a program does not have a <cli-name>.profile, you should either make a .profile which is preferred or use the COMMAND environment variable.
What should NOT be sandboxed or NOT wrapped/symlinked to /usr/local/bin/<cli-name>?
1a. Anything that could interfere with portage 1b. Anything that prevents emerge @system or the unemerging of Firejail 2a. Anything that could break init 2b. Anything that could break the display manager or login 3. Programs that use the root user 4. Anything that prevents access or the loading of the root user account 5. Anything that interferes with updating bootloader/initramfs/kernel 6. Programs that may be used in ebuilds or build scripts
Like was mentioned above, limited users should try to increase the sandbox coverage by building links/wrappers within the user's home dir.
You can use the AUTO_BLACKLIST or delete /usr/local/bin/<cli-name> if you suspect breakage in your configuration.
Next round of proposals.

use.flag: Auto add wrappers to /usr/local/bin if the program exists.
Enable support for custom AppArmor profiles
Enable chrooting to custom directory
Install contrib scripts
Enable D-Bus proxy support
Enable file transfers between sandboxes and the host system
Enable global config file
Enable support for hardened_malloc as a hardened allocator.
Enable support for the kernel's Landlock Linux Security Module (LSM).
Enable support for mimalloc as a hardened allocator.
Enable networking features
Enable overlayfs
Enable private home feature
Enable support for Scudo as a hardened allocator.
Enable system call filtering

Enable Selfrando to mitigate against ROP attacks by shuffling functions, which may contain ROP gadgets, using ChaCha20 as the PSRNG (Pseudo Random Number Generator).
If the USE flag is disabled, it will do ROP mitigations using shuffle-sections linker option. - For mold, it will use Fisher-Yates Shuffing with Xorshift PSRNG with a linear-modulo equation. - For lld, it will use std::shuffle with Mersenne Twister (mt19937) PSRNG.
Enable SELinux labeling support
Performs basic testing
Performs testing on the Firejail profiles. Expect longer testing time.
Performs testing on the X11 portions
Enable attaching a new user namespace to a sandbox (--noroot option)
Enable X11 sandboxing for GUIs in restricted untrusted mode. (It may crash programs or programs may not prefer untrusted mode.)
Enable X security extension support for windowed apps.
Enable Xpra support for windowed apps and better OpenGL support.
Enable xcsecurity support for balanced sandboxing.
Enable Xvfb support for sandboxing remote access or headless servers.
Enable Xephyr support for windowed apps.
Add USE enabled wrappers to /etc/local/bin to immediately use the program sandboxed. (EXPERIMENTAL)
